mnemonics: Downhill Skier: To minimize cost, always ski DOWN the steepest slope (the negative gradient).
keyPoints: The gradient of a cost function points in the direction of steepest ascent. To minimize the cost, we move in the opposite (negative gradient) direction.
The step size (learning rate) is a crucial hyperparameter. Too small leads to slow convergence; too large can cause overshooting and divergence.
The process involves iteratively updating the parameters: θ_new = θ_old - η * ∇J(θ), where η is the learning rate and ∇J(θ) is the gradient of the cost function.
commonMistakes: Using a fixed learning rate that is too large, causing the algorithm to oscillate or diverge instead of converging to a minimum.
Forgetting to take the negative of the gradient, which would result in ascending towards a maximum instead of descending towards a minimum.
